name: Propagate Viewport overscroll-behavior from Root
summary:
Propagate overscroll-behavior from the root instead of the body.

The CSS working group resolved[0] on not propagating  properties from the <body> to the viewport. Rather, properties of the viewport are to be propagated from the root (i.e. <html>) element e.g. scroll-behavior[1], scroll-snap-type[2], scroll-padding[3]. As such, overscroll-behavior should be propagated from the root element. However, Chrome has had a longstanding issue of propagating overscroll-behavior from the <body> rather than the root, which deviates from the behavior of Safari(WebKit) and Firefox(Gecko). This feature intends to fix this by propagating overscroll-behavior from the root rather than the <body>.

[0]  https://github.com/w3c/csswg-drafts/issues/6079#issuecomment-816307011
[1] https://drafts.csswg.org/css-overflow/#:~:text=not%20propagated%20to%20the%20viewport
[2] https://drafts.csswg.org/css-scroll-snap/#:~:text=not%20propagated%20from%20HTML%20body
[3] https://drafts.csswg.org/css-scroll-snap/#:~:text=padding%20values%20are%20not%20propagated%20from%20HTML%20body
feature_notes:
A UseCounter[1] was added for cases where there might be observable differences in behavior of Chrome should this fix be applied. As this UserCounter reports a rather high frequency (of greater than 1% of page loads), we did a manual inspection[2] of 20 of the most popular sites affected by this, comparing Chrome's behavior with and without the fix and verified that making this change is low-risk. The main form the breakage takes is allowing pull-to-refresh or backward navigation on some pages where it wasn't previously allowed or vice versa. On mobile platforms, in most of these cases, the new behavior matches the behavior of Safari and Firefox which also allow pull-to-refresh. In the one case in which this wasn't true, Safari and Firefox's behaviors didn't match due to an interaction with the overflow property - Chrome used to match Safari, but now matches firefox. 
On Desktop platforms, the new behavior on Desktop  (allowing backward navigation) matched the existing behavior of Firefox and Safari on all sites tested.
Overall, we deemed it a positive step towards interoperability with low risk of meaningful harm to users or authors.

[1] https://chromestatus.com/metrics/feature/timeline/popularity/3736
[2] https://bit.ly/4ecLqQi
creator: awogbemila@google.com
rollout_plan_displayname: Will ship enabled for all users
motivation:
Blink exhibits a behavior that differs from Gecko and WebKit in the way it propagates overscroll-behavior to the viewport. The value of this property, when propagated to the viewport, controls whether the user agent allows certain default user agent actions when the viewport is dragged/scrolled beyond its boundaries. Examples of these default actions are pull-to-refresh (typical on mobile) and swiping for forward/backward navigation (typical on Desktop).
By modifying Chrome to propagate overscroll-behavior from the root and not the body, we are bringing the 3 major engines closer to interoperable implementations of the property.
interop_compat_risks: This fixes an interop issue in which Chrome behaves differently from WebKit(Safari) and Gecko(Firefox).
comments:
A UseCounter[1] was added for cases where there might be observable differences in behavior of Chrome should this fix be applied. As this UserCounter reports a rather high frequency (of greater than 1% of page loads), we did a manual inspection[2] of 20 of the most popular sites affected by this, comparing Chrome's behavior with and without the fix and verified that making this change is low-risk. The main form the breakage takes is allowing pull-to-refresh or backward navigation on some pages where it wasn't previously allowed or vice versa. On mobile platforms, in most of these cases, the new behavior matches the behavior of Safari and Firefox which also allow pull-to-refresh. In the one case in which this wasn't true, Safari and Firefox's behaviors didn't match due to an interaction with the overflow property - Chrome used to match Safari, but now matches firefox. 
On Desktop platforms, the new behavior on Desktop  (allowing backward navigation) matched the existing behavior of Firefox and Safari on all sites tested.
Overall, we deemed it a positive step towards interoperability with low risk of meaningful harm to users or authors.

[1] https://chromestatus.com/metrics/feature/timeline/popularity/3736
[2] https://bit.ly/4ecLqQi

maturity.text: Specification currently under development in a Working Group
